3A_Bibliographic.citation: Yang, S., Guo, B., Lu, Y., Wu, X., Liu, T., Peng, Z., & Yang, B. (2022). Parental psychological problems were associated with higher screen time and the use of mature-rated media in children. Acta Paediatrica, 111(6), 1253-1261.
3A_Bibliographic.notes: Author names inferred from DOI metadata; the parsed text does not include a full author list.

3B_Sample_Data.abcd_release: Baseline data collected in 2016-2018 released for researchers in 2019
3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We used cross-sectional baseline data collected in 2016-2018 (N = 11,875) released for researchers in 2019. We included 10,650 participants who had complete information on the study variables.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Parental psychological problems (externalising, internalising, total problems) - binary clinical cutoff (T-score >= 65)
3C_Exposure.modalities: N/A - exposure is parental psychopathology, not screen time. Screen time is the outcome.
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Binary cutoff (T-score >= 65 = clinically significant problem vs. T-score < 65 = no clinically significant problem)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: The Adult Self-Report was used to examine the main caregivers' psychological problems. It is a standardised self-administered questionnaire to measure psychopathology with 123 items... Raw scores were converted into gender norm-based T-scores and participants were classified as not having a clinically significant problem (T-score <65) or as having a clinically significant problem (T-score >=65).
3C_Exposure.notes: NOTE: In this paper, screen time is the OUTCOME, not the exposure. The exposure is parental psychological problems. This is relevant to the review because it examines factors associated with children's screen time in ABCD.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Children's total daily screen time (minutes/day) and meeting <=2 hour recommendation (binary)
3D_Outcome.base_rate_if_binary: 35.9% met <=2 hours/day recommendation; 30.3% watched mature-rated movies; 36.5% played mature-rated games
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Screen time was assessed with 12 questions from the Youth Screen Time Survey developed by the ABCD study group. The questionnaire asks children to report the number of hours spent on a typical weekday and weekend day performing various recreational, non-educative, screen-based activities including television viewing, video watching (such as YouTube), playing online games, texting, visiting social networking sites (such as Twitter or Instagram), and using video chat (such as Facetime).
3D_Outcome.notes: Screen time is the outcome variable here, not the exposure. Both continuous (min/day) and binary (<=2h vs >2h) operationalizations used.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Multilevel general linear models for continuous outcomes; multilevel logistic regression for binary outcomes; study site as random intercept
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: child's age, sex, ethnicity, family income, main caregiver (mother vs other), presence of child psychological problem, family conflict (in Model 2)
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: We used multilevel general linear models to examine if presence of parental psychological problems was associated with children's daily screen time, measured as minutes per day. Multilevel logistic regression models were used to examine if presence of parental psychological problems was associated with the binary outcome of children meeting versus not meeting the recommendation of two daily hours or less. Study site was used as the random intercept and the analyses were adjusted for child's age, sex, ethnicity, family income, the main caregiver (mother for 85% vs. other for 15%) and presence of child psychological problem (83% without and 17% with psychological problem) in the first step. In the second step, we additionally adjusted for family conflict score.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Watching mature-rated movies (binary: never vs at least sometimes)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Parental internalising problems were associated with children watching mature-rated movies (odds ratio [OR] = 1.14, 95% confidence interval [CI] = 1.00, 1.30)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Playing mature-rated games (binary: never vs at least sometimes)

3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: Although the effect sizes were rather small, this study extends previous knowledge by suggesting that parental psychological problems have a role in children's screen media behaviours.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper explicitly acknowledges small effect sizes (Cohen's d between 0.09 and 0.10) multiple times and uses appropriately cautious language. Reports only threshold p-values (p<0.05, p<0.01) rather than exact values.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: For interventions and preventions, it would be important to identify the mediating factors that connect parental psychological problems with children's screen time... we suggest that parents' mental health should be considered as one possible contributor to children's screen time behaviours.
3H_Recommendations.notes: Recommendations are relatively cautious and directed toward future research and clinical consideration.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion appropriately acknowledges small effect sizes (Cohen's d 0.09-0.10) and frames findings cautiously. It distinguishes between passive (TV, video) and active (social media, gaming) screen use. The paper notes cross-sectional limitations preventing causal inference and discusses potential mechanisms (family conflict, parenting). Overall, the discussion is measured and does not overstate the clinical significance of the small associations found.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: First author name not clearly visible in parsed text; inferred from DOI. The primary association examined is parental psychopathology -> child screen time, which is a somewhat unusual direction for a screen time review.
